metadata.title: Why swapping the order of two arguments flips Claude's verdict
metadata.purpose: On frontier-of-ability calls Claude leans toward the second of two options — plausibly because in web training data the later option was more often the correct one — so option order becomes a hidden lever on close decisions.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro af_kore) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.exclusions: full CoT lesson, sampling temperature, benchmark statistics
metadata.visual_object: Two stacked reviews, identical sentences in swapped order, each with an arrow landing on the second option
metadata.hook: On a genuinely borderline judgment, presenting the same two options in the opposite order can change Claude's answer — nothing about the content moved.

beats.narration_text: A review with one positive and one negative sentence. Positive-then-negative → Claude judges it negative. Swap to negative-then-positive → Claude judges it positive. Same words, reordered; the verdict follows the second option.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: Order shouldn't change a judgment about fixed content; here it did, and the answer tracked whichever option came second; why?
On a genuinely borderline judgment, presenting the same two options in the opposite order can change Claude's answer — nothing about the content moved.

On frontier-of-ability calls Claude leans toward the second of two options — plausibly because in web training data the later option was more often the correct one — so option order becomes a hidden lever on close decisions.
beats.visual: Two stacked reviews, identical sentences in swapped order, each with an arrow landing on the second option
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: Sentences A(+) and B(−). Order A,B → verdict B. Order B,A → verdict A. Content unchanged.

On frontier-of-ability calls Claude leans toward the second of two options — plausibly because in web training data the later option was more often the correct one — so option order becomes a hidden lever on close decisions.
